title: Efficient Beam Search for Large Language ModelsUsing Trie-Based Decoding
authors:
Brian J Chan1Jui-Hung Cheng1Mao Xun Huang2Chao-Ting Chen1&Hen-Hsen Huang31Department of Computer Science, National Chengchi University, Taipei, Taiwan2Department of Information Management, National Chengchi University, Taipei, Taiwan3Institute of Information Science, Academia Sinica, Taipei, Taiwan{110703065,110703007,110306019,110703038}@nccu.edu.tw,
hhhuang@iis.sinica.edu.tw
abstract: In Transformer-based sequence-to-sequence generation, beam search has proven effective in enhancing the quality of generated sequences compared to greedy decoding. Conventional beam search methods typically adopt either a sequential or batch-based approach. The sequential approach, while memory-efficient, requires multiple decoding passes to construct a complete search tree, leading to significantly slower inference. On the other hand, the batch-based approach enables parallel computation across beams, but at the expense of high memory consumption due to the need to maintain separate key-value (KV) caches for each beam. In this study, we introduce a novel trie (prefix-tree)-based parallel decoding method that addresses the memory inefficiency of batch-based beam search. By sharing a single KV cache among all beams that share the same prefix, the proposed method not only reduces memory consumption dramatically but also enables parallel decoding across all branches. This innovative use of a prefix tree offers an efficient alternative for beam search, achieving significant memory savings while preserving inference speed, making it particularly well-suited for memory-constrained environments or large-scale model deployments.

paragraphs: Large language models (LLMs) face significant deployment challenges due to their high GPU memory requirements. For example, the 8-billion-parameter Llama 3 model, when deployed in float16 precision, requires approximately 15.7GB of GPU memory solely for its model parameters. Processing an 8k token sequence adds another 2.5GB for the key-value (KV) cache. These constraints make efficient memory utilization a critical factor in optimizing LLM performance.

Memory optimization not only reduces hardware requirements but also accelerates inference. Modern GPUs often exhibit faster computation speeds than memory transfer rates, leading to a memory-bound performance bottleneck. Addressing this bottleneck has spurred innovations like Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022; Dao, 2024), which minimizes memory operations. Efficient memory use reduces the overhead of transferring data within the GPU, enhancing both speed and scalability.

The decoding process plays a pivotal role in the performance and quality of sequence generation in LLMs. Typical decoding strategies fall into three categories: greedy decoding, top-kk sampling, and beam search. Greedy decoding selects the most probable token at each step, offering simplicity but often failing to recover from suboptimal decisions. Top-kk sampling introduces diversity by choosing the next token from the kk most probable options based on their probabilities. While effective for generating varied outputs, top-kk sampling is prone to hallucination (Dziri et al., 2021), limiting its applicability for tasks requiring high factual accuracy, such as programming, math, or retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) (Lewis et al., 2020; Pham and Vo, 2024).

Beam search strikes a balance between greedy decoding and probabilistic exploration. It maintains a fixed number of partial sequences, called beams, at each step, selecting the most promising candidates based on their cumulative log probabilities. By keeping multiple hypotheses, beam search can recover from locally suboptimal decisions, often yielding better results than greedy decoding. However, its computational cost and memory demands make it less practical for real-world applications, especially for large-scale models.

Beam search’s high memory consumption stems from its handling of KV caches. While beam search explores multiple branches originating from a shared prefix, conventional batch-based implementations allocate independent KV caches for each branch, leading to significant memory redundancy, as overlapping tokens across branches are stored multiple times. Such inefficiencies make memory optimization crucial for scalable and cost-effective deployment.

In this paper, we propose a novel trie (prefix tree)-based decoding method that significantly reduces memory usage in beam search by leveraging the hierarchical structure of shared prefixes among branches. Our approach consolidates all branches into a single shared kernel using a trie search strategy. As shown in Figure 1, this method stores only unique tokens corresponding to shared prefixes, reducing memory consumption by eliminating redundant KV cache entries. For instance, in the illustrated example, our approach requires storing only 12 tokens, compared to the 21 tokens required by conventional batch-based methods.

The idea of trie-based decoding introduces two challenges. First, tokens from different beams may inadvertently attend to one another, resulting in corrupted outputs. Second, eliminated tokens may remain in memory, contradicting the goal of efficient memory usage. To address these issues, we adapt the attention mechanism to isolate branch-specific contexts and employ dynamic pruning to remove low-probability branches, ensuring both correctness and memory efficiency. These innovations enable our approach to achieve substantial memory savings while maintaining inference speed, offering a scalable solution for deploying large language models in resource-constrained settings.

We conduct experiments to evaluate our trie-based decoding approach against greedy decoding and conventional batch-based beam search across three datasets. The results demonstrate that our method achieves comparable performance to batch-based beam search with the same beam width, while substantially reducing memory usage, particularly for larger beam widths. Based on these findings, our contributions are as follows:

1. We propose a novel trie-based decoding approach that significantly reduces memory consumption during beam search by sharing key-value caches among branches with overlapping prefixes. This efficiency becomes increasingly pronounced with larger beam widths, addressing a critical limitation of conventional batch-based beam search.

2. Our trie-based decoding approach closely mirrors the performance of conventional batch-based beam search, producing nearly identical outputs given the same beam width. By optimizing memory usage and computational efficiency, our method achieves this level of performance with significantly reduced resource consumption, particularly for larger beam widths.

3. Our trie-based decoding approach offers a more efficient and scalable way to apply beam search in practical scenarios. The implementation is publicly released as a resource.111https://github.com/SpeedReach/tridecode Unlike top-kk sampling or greedy decoding, which prioritize efficiency at the cost of reliability, our method retains the robustness and accuracy of beam search while significantly reducing its memory and computational overhead, making it suitable for real-world deployments of large language models.

The evolution of decoding methods for language models in natural language processing (NLP) has been a subject of extensive research, focusing on improving both efficiency and output quality. This section reviews key developments in decoding strategies, including beam search, sampling methods, hybrid approaches, and recent advancements in computational efficiency.

The work of Bahdanau (2014) marked a pivotal moment in NLP, introducing the attention mechanism, which allowed models to dynamically focus on relevant parts of the input sequence during generation. This breakthrough significantly improved translation quality, especially for long sentences. Notably, the study employed beam search as its decoding method—a technique that had already gained traction in statistical machine translation.

Following this milestone, beam search became the dominant decoding method, as evidenced by its use in prominent works like Vaswani et al. (2017) and Wu et al. (2016). Beam search’s ability to maintain multiple hypotheses during decoding often resulted in outputs that were more coherent and grammatically accurate, solidifying its popularity in constrained tasks.

However, as NLP applications expanded into open-ended generation tasks, the limitations of beam search, such as its tendency to produce repetitive and generic outputs, became more evident (Holtzman et al., 2019; See et al., 2019). This led to increased interest in sampling-based approaches, such as those explored by Fan et al. (2018), which prioritize diversity and creativity in generated text.

Comparative analyses of decoding strategies have highlighted the trade-offs between beam search and sampling. Ippolito et al. (2019) showed that while sampling methods generate more diverse outputs, they often compromise coherence and factual accuracy. Similarly, Massarelli et al. (2020) emphasized the susceptibility of sampling to hallucinations, contrasting this with beam search’s strengths in accuracy and faithfulness, albeit at the cost of diversity. These trade-offs have inspired hybrid approaches, such as combining initial sampling with beam search (Massarelli et al., 2020), to leverage the strengths of both methods.

While the quality of generated text has been a major focus, computational efficiency has become increasingly critical with the growth of model size and complexity. For example, Vaswani et al. (2017) proposed a high-level algorithm that reduces the beam search space by bounding the length deviation, improving both memory efficiency and speed without sacrificing output quality. While our method operates at a lower level, it can complement such techniques to further optimize memory usage.

Recent advancements have pushed efficiency boundaries even further. Miao et al. (2024) introduced an efficient method where beam search is first performed on a draft model to construct a search tree, which is then verified with the target model in a single pass. Although their approach shares conceptual similarities with our trie-based decoding method, our solution is explicitly designed for multi-round beam search, incorporating mechanisms like pruning eliminated branches and dynamically reordering the KV cache. Furthermore, our approach can be integrated with the draft model stage to achieve even greater memory savings.

A trie, or prefix tree, is a tree-based data structure designed for efficient storage and retrieval of strings based on their prefixes (Briandais, 1959; Fredkin, 1960). A trie represents common prefixes of strings as shared nodes, enabling compact storage and efficient traversal. Each node corresponds to a character, and the path from the root to any node represents a prefix of the stored string.

In the context of NLP, tries have been employed in tasks such as language modeling, dictionary construction, and decoding. Their ability to compactly represent shared prefixes makes them particularly suitable for beam search, where multiple beams often share a large number of overlapping prefixes. As decoding progresses, most beams converge on a dominant path, leading to substantial redundancy in the KV cache across different beams.

Our work leverages the trie structure to address this redundancy. By organizing beams into a prefix tree, we consolidate overlapping prefixes into a single representation, significantly reducing memory usage. This trie-based approach ensures efficient storage of shared contexts while maintaining the integrity of independent beams during decoding. It highlights the natural fit of the trie structure for optimizing beam search in large language models, where memory constraints and computational efficiency are critical.

In this section, we introduce a trie-based decoding approach that addresses these inefficiencies by consolidating overlapping prefixes into a shared representation, significantly reducing memory usage while maintaining comparable performance to traditional beam search. We outline the conventional batch-based beam search process, explain the proposed trie-based approach, and detail key innovations such as tree-based attention masking and dynamic garbage collection for efficient memory management.

subsections.title: 3.1 Batch-Based Beam Search
paragraphs: The high-level concept of batch-based beam search is outlined in Algorithm 1. For detailed implementation, readers may refer to the HuggingFace Transformers library222https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/ccbd57a8b665fbb5b1d566c0b800dc6ede509e8e/src/transformers/generation/utils.py#L2246 (Wolf et al., 2020).

In transformer-based token generation, each newly generated token attends to the KV cache of previously generated tokens. Due to the nature of matrix operations in attention mechanisms, all tokens within a sequence must share consistent hidden state dimensions. Consequently, in batched beam search, each beam maintains a separate and complete context KV cache to preserve distinct dimensional spaces.

During beam search, most candidate beams are eliminated early on as their cumulative probability scores fall outside the beam width bb. As decoding progresses, new branches predominantly grow from the single dominant path. This leads to significant redundancy, with multiple beams sharing overlapping prefixes, as illustrated in Figure 1.

subsections.title: 3.2 Trie-Based Decoding
paragraphs: Our trie-based decoding approach leverages this redundancy by merging all branches with shared prefixes into a single dimension using a prefix tree traversal, as shown in Figure 1. This eliminates the need to store duplicated tokens across beams, significantly reducing memory usage.

However, this approach introduces two challenges. First, if the merged tensor is directly processed by the language model, tokens from different branches could attend to each other, corrupting the outputs. Second, eliminated tokens would persist in the tensor, unnecessarily occupying memory and undermining the goal of memory conservation. The following subsections present our solutions to these challenges.

To further enhance efficiency, we store the search tree state on the CPU, with each node referencing a token in the KV cache. This design enables fast traversal and efficient management of the search tree during decoding.

subsections.title: 3.3 Trie Attention
paragraphs: Combining multiple sequence branches into a single dimension improves processing efficiency but risks unwanted cross-branch interactions. For example, in Figure 1, tokens like “castle” and “was,” which belong to different branches, must not influence each other during attention operations. To ensure branch independence, we construct a specialized causal attention mask that mirrors the structure of a trie, as detailed in Algorithm 3. As illustrated in Figure 2, this mask enforces that tokens attend only to other tokens within their respective branches, maintaining the integrity of each beam during the decoding process.

The attention mask is dynamically updated in two key steps. First, after selecting the top bb tokens at each decoding step, we update the mask to reflect the relationships between these tokens and their parent branches. Second, following garbage collection (Section 3.5), we regenerate the mask to account for significant changes in the KV cache, ensuring consistency with the updated search tree structure.

subsections.title: 3.4 Positional Integrity in Trie Attention
paragraphs: In the trie-based beam search illustration, the renumbered position IDs are designed to simulate the exact behavior of conventional beam search, where the position ID of each token plays a crucial role in contextual understanding within the transformer architecture. Unlike conventional beam search, where each beam maintains independent position IDs, our trie-based approach merges shared prefixes and consolidates the tokens from all beams into a single structure. However, neighboring tokens within the same branch may be separated by tokens from other branches in this shared structure.

To preserve the integrity of positional information, the renumbered position IDs in our approach are carefully assigned to match the positions in the original beam search. This ensures that the contextual relationship between tokens in the same branch remains intact, even when tokens from different branches are interleaved. By aligning the position IDs with those in the conventional beam search, our trie-based method achieves identical contextual understanding while maintaining memory efficiency. This alignment is critical for ensuring that the model generates outputs consistent with the original beam search behavior, while leveraging the benefits of the trie structure to reduce redundancy.

subsections.title: 3.5 Garbage Collection
paragraphs: Garbage collection is a common memory management strategy that consolidates and reclaims unused memory. In this work, because memory operations on the GPU are time-intensive, we minimize overhead by deferring the removal of unused tokens and the reorganization of the KV cache. Instead of performing these operations every decoding step, we accumulate changes over multiple rounds. After reaching a threshold, we scan the search tree references stored on the CPU, identify the KV cache positions to retain, and reorganize them in a single operation.

This approach significantly reduces the frequency of GPU memory operations, optimizing overall efficiency. For example, as shown in Figure 1, tokens like “far” and “a” are marked for removal during this process, conserving memory without frequent interruptions.

subsections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
paragraphs: We evaluated our trie-based decoding approach across three tasks using varying beam widths b∈{1,3,9,15}b\in\{1,3,9,15\}. The backbone LLM for all experiments was Phi-3.5-mini-instruct (Abdin et al., 2024). The tasks included: (1) code generation on the HumanEval dataset (Chen et al., 2021), (2) news summarization on the CNN/DailyMail dataset (Hermann et al., 2015), and (3) domain-specific summarization on the QMSUM dataset (Query-based Multi-domain Meeting Summarization) (Zhong et al., 2021). For both summarization tasks, performance was measured using the ROUGE-L score (Lin, 2004), while code generation was evaluated by accuracy, where higher scores indicate solving more problems.

In addition to performance metrics, we evaluated memory and time efficiency. Memory efficiency is measured as peak memory per token (Mem/Tok), calculated by subtracting the model’s static memory footprint from the peak memory and dividing by the combined length of the input and the output:

Here, the model memory refers to the fixed GPU memory allocation required when loading the model, which remains constant across all experiments and is therefore excluded from comparisons. Time efficiency is measured as generation speed in tokens per second (Tok/Sec), calculated as the output length divided by the total inference time. This metric indicates the throughput of the model during text generation.

Although we tested our approach on a single LLM (Phi-3.5-mini-instruct), it is important to note that our trie-based decoding method is model-agnostic and universally applicable to any multi-head attention architecture. This ensures that the benefits of our approach, including reduced memory consumption and improved computational efficiency, can be extended to other LLMs with minimal modifications.

All experiments were conducted on a cluster of 8 Tesla V100-SXM2-32GB GPUs. We benchmarked our method against the HuggingFace Transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020), comparing computational efficiency (execution time) and memory utilization. Memory usage was measured using torch.cuda.memory_allocated().

Our decoding approach was compared to both the greedy decoding method and the conventional batch-based beam search used in the HuggingFace framework. In terms of performance scores, the greedy approach is equivalent to beam search with a beam width of 1 (i.e., b=1b=1). For beam widths b>1b>1, our trie-based decoding approach is designed to produce equivalent output sequences to traditional batch-based beam search, achieving nearly identical performance scores while significantly reducing memory consumption and maintaining comparable computational efficiency.

subsections.title: 4.2 Results and Discussion
paragraphs: The experimental results, summarized in Table 1, demonstrate the effectiveness of our trie-based decoding approach across the three tasks. Our approach was evaluated for memory efficiency, generation speed, and output performance, and compared against both greedy decoding and conventional batch-based beam search across multiple beam widths (b∈{1,3,9,15}b\in\{1,3,9,15\}). For QMSUM, we cannot report the performance of the conventional beam search with b=15b=15 because of the memory limitation.

Memory Efficiency: Our trie-based decoding drastically reduces memory consumption per token (Mem/Tok) compared to batch-based beam search, especially for larger beam widths where memory redundancy is more pronounced. For instance, on CNN/DM with b=15b=15, our method achieves a Mem/Tok of 1.55—92% lower than the 19.95 required by batch-based beam search. On QMSUM with b=9b=9, Mem/Tok is reduced from 27.14 to 3.27. Furthermore, while batch-based beam search runs out of memory at b=15b=15 on QMSUM, our approach completes the task with only 1.57 GB per token. By eliminating redundant KV cache for shared prefixes, our method is not only highly memory-efficient but also scalable for memory-constrained environments.

Generation Speed: Our approach maintains competitive generation speeds (Tok/Sec) while delivering significant memory savings. On QMSUM and HumanEval, our method consistently matches or slightly exceeds the generation speed of batch-based beam search across all beam widths. These results demonstrate that our trie-based decoding improves memory efficiency without introducing much computational overhead, making it suitable for real-time applications.

Output Quality: Our trie-based decoding achieves performance scores that are nearly identical to those of batch-based beam search for the same beam width, as expected from their theoretical equivalence. For example, on CNN/DM and QMSUM, both methods achieve very similar ROUGE scores, and on HumanEval. Statistical significance tests confirmed that these small differences in performance are not statistically significant across all datasets (p>0.05p>0.05).

The slight performance differences observed can be attributed to subtle implementation variances and unavoidable randomness333https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/randomness.html inherent in the decoding process, such as the order of operations during parallel processing. Despite these minor discrepancies, our trie-based decoding delivers high-quality outputs that are nearly indistinguishable from those produced by conventional batch-based beam search. However, this equivalence in output quality is achieved with a huge reduction in memory usage.

Scalability across Beam Widths: The benefits of our trie-based approach become increasingly pronounced as the beam width increases. For larger bb, conventional batch-based beam search incurs steep increases in memory consumption, while our method maintains consistently low memory usage. For example, on HumanEval with b=15b=15, our method achieves a Mem/Tok of 3.31 compared to 16.16 for batch-based beam search. This scalability makes our approach advantageous for resource-constrained environments where large beam widths are required to achieve high-quality outputs.

Memory Usage vs. Length of Generated Text: The memory usage trends in Figure 3 illustrate the relationship between memory consumption and the length of generated text, highlighting the efficiency of our trie-based decoding approach compared to batch-based beam search. Across all datasets and beam widths, our method consistently uses significantly less memory, with the efficiency gains becoming more pronounced as the length of the generated text increases. For instance, at higher beam widths (b=15b=15), batch-based beam search exhibits steep memory growth due to the need to maintain separate key-value caches for each beam, while our trie-based approach maintains a much more gradual increase by consolidating shared prefixes.

This memory efficiency is particularly notable in tasks involving longer input sequences. These results demonstrate that our trie-based approach not only minimizes memory overhead but also scales effectively with increasing text lengths, making it an ideal solution for long-form text generation in resource-constrained environments.

By significantly reducing memory usage, maintaining competitive generation speeds, and achieving comparable performance scores, our method addresses critical inefficiencies in traditional beam search. These advantages make it a practical solution for deploying large language models in memory-constrained and real-time applications.

Memory Spike: As shown in Figure 3, both batch-based and trie-based decoding exhibit a memory spike during the prefilling stage. Where bb represents the beam width, the batch-based approach creates a large tensor of size [bb, input_length, vocab_size=32064], while the trie-based approach creates a smaller tensor [1, input_length, vocab_size=32064]. After selecting the top bb candidates for the first token, memory usage drops significantly in both approaches as the output tensor shrinks to [bb, 1, vocab_size] for following decoding steps.

Our approach, trie-based decoding for LLMs, is a novel beam search method that significantly reduces memory usage by efficiently consolidating shared prefixes across beams. This innovation minimizes redundant memory consumption while maintaining comparable computational performance. The scalability of our method is particularly evident in tasks with large context lengths and wide beam widths, such as programming tasks requiring high precision (e.g., HumanEval), where it outperforms conventional methods with minimal overhead. Our method’s precision and efficiency make it a preferred choice for complex and objective language tasks. Moreover, our approach requires no additional training or specialized hardware, making it a cost-effective and practical solution for deploying LLMs in resource-constrained environments, ensuring robust performance across diverse applications.

This work was conducted using existing, publicly available, and widely recognized datasets, including CNN/DM, QMSUM, and HumanEval. These datasets are commonly used benchmarks in the research community and do not pose any ethical concerns regarding their use.

On the other hand, our proposed trie-based decoding approach significantly enhances computational efficiency by reducing memory usage and maintaining performance equivalent to conventional beam search. By enabling more efficient utilization of computational resources, our work contributes positively to environmental sustainability by lowering the energy consumption of large language model inference. Furthermore, the scalability and cost-effectiveness of our method promote broader accessibility to advanced language models, fostering social benefits through the democratization of AI technologies.

This work was partially supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC), Taiwan, under the grant 112-2221-E-001-016-MY3, by Academia Sinica, under the grant 236d-1120205, and by National Center for High-performance Computing (NCHC), National Applied Research Laboratories (NARLabs), and NSTC under the project “Taiwan’s 113th year endeavoring in the promotion of a trustworthy generative AI large language model and the cultivation of literacy capabilities (Trustworthy AI Dialog Engine, TAIDE)”.
